The paper search wasn't relevant, but the physical principle is well-established in the amyloid literature (stoichiometric inhibitors that sequester monomer or cap fibril ends reduce the final aggregate mass; sub-stoichiometric ones mainly change kinetics). The breakthrough idea is sound and physically motivated. Data is normalized globally, so inhibited curves genuinely saturate below 1.0 in the response table — a constant plateau forces them all to the same ceiling, inflating NMSE.

I'll implement the curve-dependent plateau exactly as described, keeping the rate structure unchanged and moving to `constant_symbols(11)`.

nstants (< 13), one `evaluate_expression` call, straight-line code, and no gated/singular constructs. The plateau `c0/(1+c9²·cd) + c10²·m0` is smooth and strictly positive over the observed range, and the `(plateau − c)` factor self-limits integration. The initial values start the plateau near `1.0` (`c9=0.1` → near-zero suppression, `c10=0.0` → no monomer term initially), letting least-squares discover whether inhibitor-dependent capacity reduction improves the fit on strongly-inhibited curves.
